---
title: "SMARCE1 RNA-seq: Differential Expression Report"
author: "Glady Hazitha Samuel"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true       # Back to true, OR remove this line entirely
    code-tools: false     # Change to false - hides the code button
    echo: false           # hides all code by default
    message: false        # hides all messages by default
    warning: false        # hides all warnings by default
---



# Introduction

## Background on SMARCE1 

Clear cell meningiomas are rare and aggressive brain tumors that are characterized by loss of function mutations in SMARCE1 [@StPierre2022; @Smith2013]. SMARCE1 is a key component of the BAF complex, a chromosome remodelling complex [@KadochCrabtree2015]. During transcription, the BAF complex regulates gene expression by altering chromosome structure to allow or restrict access to transcription factors, enhancers and other molecules involved in transcription [@KadochCrabtree2015]. Mutations in components of the BAF complex are often associated with cancers [@Shain2013]. Although many of the components of the BAF complex have been well studied, the specific role of SMARCE1 in transcriptional regulation and gene expression is unclear. ![](smarce1.png){fig-align="center" width="70%" fig-cap="SMARCE1 clear cell meningioma illustration [@Bogershausen2018]."}

In this Nature Genetics publication, the authors study the role of SMARCE1 in context of the BAF complex [@StPierre2022]. Here the authors use various analysis including RNA-seq, ATAC-seq and ChIP-seq to understand this pathway and possible pharmaceutical interventions.

## Data and Study Design

This study uses a combination of RNA-seq, ATAC-seq and ChIP-seq data to study the mechanism by which loss of SMARCE1 results in tumorogenesis. The authors performed comprehensive transcriptomic analysis of SMARCE Null cells compared to WT controls in AC7 arachnoid cells which are used as a meningioma model. They also looked at NF2 deficient AC7 cells which are involved in many fibrous meningiomas.In this project, I specifically focus on the RNASeq analysis and study the key biological pathways involved with loss of SMARCE1 and NF2 to look at pathways that are shared and distinct between the two conditions.The study includes libraries prepared using Illumina Tru-Seq Stranded mRNA Library Prep Kit. Using a AC7 meningioma cell model, clones were established for WT(3 clones-2 replicates each – 6 biological replicates) and SMARCE1 Null(2 Clones- 2 replicates each = 4 biological replicates.). The reads are single end reads of 75bp length sequenced on Illumina NextSeq 500.

## RNA-seq Workflow

The authors of this paper used STAR and the hg19 human gene annotation file for alignments. The output gene count tables which were then loaded into EdgeR (v.3.12.1) to evaluate differential gene expression. Downstream, the EdgeR log2Fold changes were used for GSEA analysis using the GSEAPreranked Tool (GSEA63). For enrichment of select genes, Metscape or Hypergeomatric tests were used to see which pathways were enriched amongst the select subset of genes. Heatmaps were made based on RPKM values which were convered to z-scores. PCA was performed to show sample level differences using functions from the corpcor package in R. 


## Results
The PCA analysis of the samples show a strong separation between the NF2 null and the SMARCE1 null gene expression. In comparison to the WT, the authors found 3456 differentially expressed genes in SMARCE1 Null cells. Of these, 1684 were significantly downregulated and 1772 were upregulated. The paper does not list the numbers for differentially expressed genes in the NF2 null cells. However, in a combined heatmap, the figure shows that 4226 genes were significantly differentially expressed in either SMARCE1 null of NF2 null compared to WT. The full list of differentially expressed genes is not available however, figures show representative genes and categories associated with each condition. These genes are differentially expressed in both Smarce Null and NF2 null compared to WT: FLNA, SOD2, CREB1, SKP2 and STXBP1. 


# RNA-seq Analysis

## QC and Alignment


For the intital QC analysis, Fastqc and Multiqc analysis were performed the for single read RNA-seq libraries from the publication. The total number of sequences per sample ranged form 14 million to 60 millions reads. The mean quality phred scores were above 30 for all samples. 4 samples failed the per base sequence content and 1 sample failed the per base GC content. The duplication levels were also high and 36/46 samples failed according to the multiqc. However on further research in the literature, this seems normal for RNAseq data. Overall, the adapter content was low for all samples and all samples passed QC. However, since a few samples showed slight increase in adapters in the 3’ end, I performed adapter trimming. After trimming, the QC report showed the expected improvements. Adapter content was mostly eliminated across all samples, and the per-base quality profile became much more uniform along the read length. Overrepresented sequences also decreased substantially after trimming, as well as the GC content. The main metric that remained unchanged was the duplication levels. Overall, the trimming step effectively addressed the issues I saw in the raw data. This publication did not go into details regarding the initial QC and trimming steps etc. This could be because it included a variety of omics analysis including RNAseq, CHIPseq ,ATACseq and scRNAseq which could be why they did not emphasize the analysis details/methods for RNAseq.


## DESEQ2

```{r packages}

#| include: false
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# CRAN packages
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cran.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("ggrepel", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("ggrepel", repos = "https://cran.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("pheatmap", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("pheatmap", repos = "https://cran.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("ashr", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("ashr", repos = "https://cran.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)

# Bioconductor packages
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("BiocManager", repos = "https://cran.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("DESeq2", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("DESeq2", ask = FALSE, update = FALSE, quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("biomaRt", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("biomaRt", ask = FALSE, update = FALSE, quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("goseq", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("goseq", ask = FALSE, update = FALSE, quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("apeglm", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("apeglm", ask = FALSE, update = FALSE, quiet = TRUE)
if (!requireNamespace("ggridges", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("ggridges", repos = "https://cran.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)

# Load libraries
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library("tidyverse")
  library("DESeq2")
  library("ggrepel")
  library("apeglm")
  library("ashr")
  library("pheatmap")
  library("biomaRt")
  library("goseq")
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggridges)
})
```

## Import Count Data

The gene count data from HTSeq were imported and organized into a DESeqDataSet with the experimental design ~ condition to enable differential expression testing between SMARCE1-null, NF2-null, and wild-type samples.

```{r counts}


#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

####################################################### 
# Point R to count data, set an output file prefix 
####################################################### 

# create an object with the directory containing your counts:
	# !!edit this to point to your own count file directory!!
directory <- "../../results/04_counts/counts"

# ensure the count files are where you think they are
list.files(directory)

sampleFiles <- list.files(directory, pattern = ".*counts$")

####################################################### 
# Read the count data into R along with treatment information
####################################################### 

# load in metadata table
meta <- read.csv("../../metadata/kadoch_metadata.csv") %>%
	dplyr::select(sample, condition, clone) 

# ensure that sampleFiles and metadata table are in the same order
all(str_remove(sampleFiles, ".counts") == meta$Run)

# now create a data frame with sample names, file names and treatment information. 
sampleTable <- data.frame(
	sampleName = meta$sample,
	fileName = sampleFiles,
	condition = meta$condition
	)

# look at the data frame to ensure it is what you expect:
sampleTable

# create the DESeq data object
ddsHTSeq <- DESeqDataSetFromHTSeqCount(
		sampleTable = sampleTable, 
		directory = directory, 
		design = ~condition
		)

```


## Quality Control and Filtering

Wild-type was set as the reference condition for all comparisons. Genes with < than 50 total counts across all samples were filtered out to reduce noise and improve statistical power. The histogram shows the distribution of the log transformed counts. 

```{r factors}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Reset treatment factors
####################################################### 

# factor order determines reference level (i.e. control vs treatment)
	# factors are automatically ordered alphabetically
	# we want WT as the reference level

# To see the levels as they are now:
#ddsHTSeq$condition

# To replace the order with one of your choosing, create a vector with the order you want:
# NEW (sets WT as reference):
treatments <- c("WT", "SmarceNull", "NF2Null")

# Then reset the factor levels:
ddsHTSeq$condition <- factor(ddsHTSeq$condition, levels = treatments)

# verify the order
#ddsHTSeq$condition

####################################################### 
# Filter out genes with very low expression
####################################################### 

# what does expression look like across genes?

# sum counts for each gene across samples
sumcounts <- rowSums(counts(ddsHTSeq))

# take the log
logsumcounts <- log(sumcounts, base = 10)

# plot a histogram of the log scaled counts
hist(logsumcounts, breaks = 100)

# you can see the typically high dynamic range of RNA-Seq, with a mode in the 
# distribution around 1000 fragments per gene, but some genes up over 1 million fragments. 

# get genes with summed counts greater than 50
keep <- sumcounts > 50

# keep only the genes for which the vector "keep" is TRUE
ddsHTSeq <- ddsHTSeq[keep, ]

```

## DESEQ dds
Differential expression testing was performed using DESeq2, which models count data with a negative binomial distribution to account for biological and technical variability. The pipeline estimates normalization factors, dispersion parameters, and performs statistical testing to identify genes with significant expression changes between conditions

```{r statistics}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

####################################################### 
# Run the statistical analysis
######################################################

dds <- DESeq(ddsHTSeq)

```
 
## Differentially Expressed Genes

The Differential expression analysis identified 8,037 genes significantly changed in SMARCE1-null cells and 4,067 genes in NF2-null cells (adjusted p-value < 0.1) as seen in the table below. SMARCE1 loss affected nearly twice as many genes as NF2 loss, suggesting a broader transcriptional impact.
In the SMARCE1-responsive genes, 3,985 were upregulated and 4,052 were downregulated,showing a balanced transcriptional response with slightly more gene repression. For NF2, 2,103 genes were upregulated and 1,964 downregulated, showing a similar balance between activation and repression. The comparable ratios  up and downregulated genes in both conditions suggest that loss of either SMARCE1 or NF2 triggers both activating and repressive transcriptional programs, rather than a unidirectional effect.


```{r results}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

####################################################### 
# Which results do we want?
####################################################### 
# we are interested in:
	# 1) genes responding to SMARCE1 knockout vs WT
	# 2) genes responding to NF2 knockout vs WT

# Extract results for each comparison
res1 <- results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "SmarceNull", "WT"))
res2 <- results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "NF2Null", "WT"))

####################################################### 
# Quickly summarize results
####################################################### 
# get a quick summary of the tables
summary(res1)
summary(res2)

# Create summary table of DE genes
de_summary <- data.frame(
  Condition = c("SMARCE1-null vs WT", "NF2-null vs WT"),
  `Total DE genes (padj < 0.1)` = c(
    sum(res1$padj < 0.1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(res2$padj < 0.1, na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  `Upregulated` = c(
    sum(res1$padj < 0.1 & res1$log2FoldChange > 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(res2$padj < 0.1 & res2$log2FoldChange > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  `Downregulated` = c(
    sum(res1$padj < 0.1 & res1$log2FoldChange < 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(res2$padj < 0.1 & res2$log2FoldChange < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

# Display the summary table
knitr::kable(de_summary, 
             caption = "Summary of differentially expressed genes (padj < 0.1)",
             align = c('l', 'c', 'c', 'c'))
```


## DESEQ2 LFC shrinkage
 
In order to get more accurate effect size estimates for visualizations, a log2 fold change shrinkage method called ashr was used. This improves individual gene-level estimates by reducing noise for downstream visualizations and analysis such as GSEA. 
 
```{r shrink}

#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include:false
####################################################### 
# Get a table of shrunken log2 fold changes
####################################################### 

# get shrunken log fold changes for SMARCE1
res_shrink1 <- lfcShrink(dds, type = "ashr", coef = "condition_SmarceNull_vs_WT")

# get shrunken log fold changes for NF2
res_shrink2 <- lfcShrink(dds, type = "ashr", coef = "condition_NF2Null_vs_WT")

# plot the shrunken log2 fold changes against the raw changes for SMARCE1:
	data.frame(l2fc = res1$log2FoldChange, l2fc_shrink = res_shrink1$log2FoldChange, padj = res1$padj) %>%
		filter(l2fc > -5 & l2fc < 5 & l2fc_shrink > -5 & l2fc_shrink < 5) %>%
		ggplot(aes(x = l2fc, y = l2fc_shrink, color = padj > 0.1)) +
			geom_point(size = .25) + 
			geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "gray") +
			ggtitle("SMARCE1: Shrunken vs Raw log2FC")

# plot the shrunken log2 fold changes against the raw changes for NF2:
	data.frame(l2fc = res2$log2FoldChange, l2fc_shrink = res_shrink2$log2FoldChange, padj = res2$padj) %>%
		filter(l2fc > -5 & l2fc < 5 & l2fc_shrink > -5 & l2fc_shrink < 5) %>%
		ggplot(aes(x = l2fc, y = l2fc_shrink, color = padj > 0.1)) +
			geom_point(size = .25) + 
			geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "gray") +
			ggtitle("NF2: Shrunken vs Raw log2FC")

# get the top 20 genes by shrunken log2 fold change for SMARCE1
	# this will include genes with outliers
#arrange(data.frame(res_shrink1), -abs(log2FoldChange)) %>% head(., n = 20)

# get the top 20 genes by shrunken log2 fold change for NF2
#arrange(data.frame(res_shrink2), -abs(log2FoldChange)) %>% head(., n = 20)

```

## MA PLOTS

MA plots visualize the relationship between mean normalized count and log2 fold change. Significantly differentially expressed genes (padj < 0.1) are highlighted in blue. Based on the plots below we can see that the shrinkage is working correctly. 


```{r maplots}


#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include:false
####################################################### 
# Data visualization
####################################################### 

# MA plot for SMARCE1
plotMA(res1, ylim = c(-4, 4), main = "SMARCE1 vs WT - Raw")
plotMA(res_shrink1, ylim = c(-4, 4), main = "SMARCE1 vs WT - Shrunken")

# MA plot for NF2
plotMA(res2, ylim = c(-4, 4), main = "NF2 vs WT - Raw")
plotMA(res_shrink2, ylim = c(-4, 4), main = "NF2 vs WT - Shrunken")

```

Ensembl gene IDs were annotated with human gene symbols using the org.Hs.eg.db database to facilitate biological interpretation of results.

```{r genesymbols}
#| include: false

# Check if ann exists, if not create it
if (!exists("ann")) {
  library(org.Hs.eg.db)
  library(AnnotationDbi)
  
  genes_clean <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res_shrink1))
  
  ann <- AnnotationDbi::select(org.Hs.eg.db,
                                keys = genes_clean,
                                columns = c("ENSEMBL", "SYMBOL", "GENENAME"),
                                keytype = "ENSEMBL")
  
  ann <- ann[!duplicated(ann$ENSEMBL), ]
  colnames(ann) <- c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_symbol", "description")
}

# Merge gene symbols into res_shrink1
res_shrink1_df <- as.data.frame(res_shrink1)
res_shrink1_df$ensembl_gene_id <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res_shrink1_df))

res_shrink1_ann <- merge(res_shrink1_df, 
                         ann[, c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_symbol")],
                         by = "ensembl_gene_id",
                         all.x = TRUE)

# Merge gene symbols into res_shrink2
res_shrink2_df <- as.data.frame(res_shrink2)
res_shrink2_df$ensembl_gene_id <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res_shrink2_df))

res_shrink2_ann <- merge(res_shrink2_df, 
                         ann[, c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_symbol")],
                         by = "ensembl_gene_id",
                         all.x = TRUE)
```

## Volcano Plot

Volcano plots visualize the relationship between log2 fold change and -log10 adjusted p-value for all genes. Genes are color-coded by significance where red indicates both statistically significant (padj < 0.1) and biologically meaningful (|log2FC| > 1) changes, while gray represents non-significant genes. The top 15 most significant genes are labeled with gene symbols. SMARCE1 loss affected 8,037 genes while NF2 loss affected 4,067 genes, with both conditions showing similar amounts of up and downregulated genes.


```{r volcano-plots}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-cap: "Volcano plots comparing differential gene expression"

library(EnhancedVolcano)
library(patchwork)

# SMARCE1 Volcano
p1 <- EnhancedVolcano(res_shrink1_ann,
                lab = res_shrink1_ann$gene_symbol,
                x = 'log2FoldChange',
                y = 'padj',
                title = 'SMARCE1-null vs WT',
                subtitle = bquote(italic("n = 8,037 DE genes (padj < 0.1)")),
                pCutoff = 0.1,
                FCcutoff = 1.0,
                pointSize = 1.0,
                labSize = 3.5,
                colAlpha = 0.4,
                legendPosition = 'bottom',
                drawConnectors = TRUE,
                widthConnectors = 0.3,
                max.overlaps = 15)

# NF2 Volcano
p2 <- EnhancedVolcano(res_shrink2_ann,
                lab = res_shrink2_ann$gene_symbol,
                x = 'log2FoldChange',
                y = 'padj',
                title = 'NF2-null vs WT',
                subtitle = bquote(italic("n = 4,067 DE genes (padj < 0.1)")),
                pCutoff = 0.1,
                FCcutoff = 1.0,
                pointSize = 1.0,
                labSize = 3.5,
                colAlpha = 0.4,
                legendPosition = 'bottom',
                drawConnectors = TRUE,
                widthConnectors = 0.3,
                max.overlaps = 15)

p1
p2
```

## Principal Component Analysis 

PCA is an important QC figure and it shows the overall relationships of the samples based on gene expression clustering. 
In our analysis, the first two principal componenets captured about 69% of the variability in gene expression. PC1 separates the WT from the SMARCE1 null while the NF2 null samples are positioned between the WT and SMARCE1 Null, suggesting a gene expression signature that is  intermediate between WT and SMARCE1. 

```{r pca}
############## PCA plot

# normalized, variance-stabilized transformed counts for visualization
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)
plotPCA(vsd, intgroup = "condition")

# alternatively, using ggplot
dat <- plotPCA(vsd, returnData = TRUE, intgroup = "condition")
p <- ggplot(dat, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, col = condition))
p <- p + geom_point(size = 3) + 
	xlab(paste("PC1: ", round(attr(dat, "percentVar")[1], 2) * 100, "% variation explained", sep = "")) + 
	ylab(paste("PC2: ", round(attr(dat, "percentVar")[2], 2) * 100, "% variation explained", sep = "")) +
	geom_label_repel(aes(label = name))
p

```

## Heatmap Visualization

To visualize expression patterns of the most differentially expressed genes, heatmaps were generated of the top 30 genes ranked by absolute log2 fold change. Gene expression was displayed using regularized log-transformed (rlog) counts, which stabilize variance across different expression levels.

Three versions of the heatmap were created: 
1. Raw rlog counts : Shows absolute expression levels across all samples
2. baseMean-scaled : Normalizes by average expression to emphasize relative changes
3. WT-scaled : Centers expression relative to wild-type samples to highlight deviations from control

From the plots below, we see that the SMARCE1 clustering is distinct from the WT , while NF2 shows an intermediate clustering which falls between WT and SMARCE1 for the top regulated genes. The plots also shows that the genes are both up and downregulated in the knockdown, suggesting there is an overall transcriptional change which is not directional. 

```{r heatmaps-smarce}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-cap: "Heatmaps of top 30 differentially expressed genes in SMARCE1-null cells"

# regularized log transformation of counts
rld <- rlog(dds, blind = FALSE)

# order gene names by absolute value of shrunken log2 fold change
lfcorder1 <- data.frame(res_shrink1) %>%
  filter(!is.na(padj)) %>% 
  arrange(-abs(log2FoldChange)) %>%
  rownames() 

# create a metadata data frame to add to the heatmaps
df <- data.frame(condition = colData(dds)[, "condition"])  
rownames(df) <- colnames(dds)

# use regularized log-scaled counts
pheatmap(
  assay(rld)[lfcorder1[1:20], ],
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  show_rownames = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  annotation_col = df,
  fontsize_row = 6,
  main = "Top 20 DE genes: SMARCE1 vs WT"
)

# re-scale by baseMean
pheatmap(
  assay(rld)[lfcorder1[1:20], ] - log(res1[lfcorder1[1:20], "baseMean"], 2),
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  show_rownames = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  annotation_col = df,
  fontsize_row = 6,
  main = "Top 20 DE genes: SMARCE1 vs WT (baseMean scaled)"
)

# re-scale by WT
pheatmap(
  assay(rld)[lfcorder1[1:20], ] - rowMeans(assay(rld)[lfcorder1[1:20], dds$condition == "WT"]),
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  show_rownames = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  annotation_col = df,
  fontsize_row = 6,
  main = "Top 20 DE genes: SMARCE1 vs WT (WT scaled)"
)
```

Heatmaps were also generated for the NF2 null vs WT comparison to visualize the top 20 genes in each condition. 

```{r heatmaps-nf2}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-cap: "Heatmaps of top 30 differentially expressed genes in NF2-null cells"

# order gene names by absolute value of shrunken log2 fold change
lfcorder2 <- data.frame(res_shrink2) %>%
  filter(!is.na(padj)) %>% 
  arrange(-abs(log2FoldChange)) %>%
  rownames() 

# use regularized log-scaled counts
pheatmap(
  assay(rld)[lfcorder2[1:20], ],
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  show_rownames = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  annotation_col = df,
  fontsize_row = 6,
  main = "Top 20 DE genes: NF2 vs WT"
)

# re-scale by baseMean
pheatmap(
  assay(rld)[lfcorder2[1:20], ] - log(res2[lfcorder2[1:20], "baseMean"], 2),
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  show_rownames = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  annotation_col = df,
  fontsize_row = 6,
  main = "Top 20 DE genes: NF2 vs WT (baseMean scaled)"
)

# re-scale by WT
pheatmap(
  assay(rld)[lfcorder2[1:20], ] - rowMeans(assay(rld)[lfcorder2[1:20], dds$condition == "WT"]),
  cluster_rows = TRUE,
  show_rownames = TRUE,
  cluster_cols = TRUE,
  annotation_col = df,
  fontsize_row = 6,
  main = "Top 20 DE genes: NF2 vs WT (WT scaled)"
)
```

## Top Genes Expression QC

These individual gene expression plots show the most significantly expressed gene in each condition.  
For SMARCE1 null, the top differentially expressed gene shows consistent expression changes across all SMARCE1-null replicates, with tight within-group clustering and clear separation from wild-type controls. This shows that the statistical significance reflects real biological differences.
The tight clustering of replicates and clear separation between conditions for these top genes suggests taht the  broader differential expression results represent true biological responses to SMARCE1 or NF2 loss, rather than technical noise or batch effects.


```{r topgenes}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
############### plot counts for individual genes

# plot the top DE gene for SMARCE1
top_gene1 <- rownames(res_shrink1)[which.min(res_shrink1$padj)]
plotCounts(dds, gene = top_gene1, intgroup = "condition", main = paste("SMARCE1 top gene:", top_gene1))

# plot the top DE gene for NF2
top_gene2 <- rownames(res_shrink2)[which.min(res_shrink2$padj)]
plotCounts(dds, gene = top_gene2, intgroup = "condition", main = paste("NF2 top gene:", top_gene2))

```



```{r annotations}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
####################################################### 
# Get gene annotations using org.Hs.eg.db
####################################################### 

library("org.Hs.eg.db")
library("AnnotationDbi")
library("dplyr")

# Remove version numbers from Ensembl IDs
genes_clean <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res1))

# Get gene symbols and descriptions - use AnnotationDbi::select explicitly
ann <- AnnotationDbi::select(org.Hs.eg.db,
                              keys = genes_clean,
                              columns = c("ENSEMBL", "SYMBOL", "GENENAME"),
                              keytype = "ENSEMBL")

# Remove duplicates, keep first entry for each gene
ann <- ann[!duplicated(ann$ENSEMBL), ]

# Rename to match HTSeq code expectations
colnames(ann) <- c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_symbol", "description")

# Check what we got
head(ann)
dim(ann)
```



```{r genelist}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
####################################################### 
# Prepare gene lists for GO enrichment
####################################################### 

library("clusterProfiler")
library("org.Hs.eg.db")

# Get significant genes for SMARCE1 (padj < 0.1)
sig_genes_smarce <- rownames(res1)[which(res1$padj < 0.1)]

# Get significant genes for NF2 (padj < 0.1)
sig_genes_nf2 <- rownames(res2)[which(res2$padj < 0.1)]

# Remove version numbers from Ensembl IDs (e.g., ENSG00000000003.17 -> ENSG00000000003)
sig_genes_smarce_clean <- sub("\\..*", "", sig_genes_smarce)
sig_genes_nf2_clean <- sub("\\..*", "", sig_genes_nf2)

# Check what we have
cat("Number of significant genes - SMARCE1:", length(sig_genes_smarce_clean), "\n")
cat("Number of significant genes - NF2:", length(sig_genes_nf2_clean), "\n")

head(sig_genes_smarce_clean)
head(sig_genes_nf2_clean)
```

## GO Analysis

Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis identified the biological processes significantly overrepresented among differentially expressed genes in each knockout condition.

According to the plots below, SMARCE1 loss primarily affects developmental and structural processes wuch as skeletal development, tissue morphogenesis, and extracellular matrix metabolism. The enrichment of negative regulation of cell proliferation suggests disruption of growth control mechanisms whic is consistent with tumor development. These pathways fit with SMARCE1's role in chromatin remodeling during development.

NF2 loss activates immune and inflammatory signaling pathways—defense responses, cytokine production, and cellular signaling through protein phosphorylation. This is very different from SMARCE1, suggesting that NF2 mutations trigger an immune response and not developmental dysregulation. 

These completely different GO profiles suggest that these two genes drive tumorgenesis through different mechanisms. This also fits with the fact that they are associated with different subtypes of meningiomas. 

```{r goanalysis}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: hide
####################################################### 
# GO enrichment - All ontologies combined (matches HTSeq)
####################################################### 

# Define background universe
universe <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res1))

# For SMARCE1 - ALL ontologies
ego_smarce_all <- enrichGO(
  gene = sig_genes_smarce_clean,
  universe = universe,
  OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
  keyType = "ENSEMBL",
  ont = "ALL",  # All ontologies combined
  pAdjustMethod = "BH",
  pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
  qvalueCutoff = 0.2
)

# For NF2 - ALL ontologies
ego_nf2_all <- enrichGO(
  gene = sig_genes_nf2_clean,
  universe = universe,
  OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
  keyType = "ENSEMBL",
  ont = "ALL",  # All ontologies combined
  pAdjustMethod = "BH",
  pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
  qvalueCutoff = 0.2
)

# View results
#cat("\nSMARCE1 - Number of enriched GO terms (all ontologies):", nrow(ego_smarce_all), "\n")


#cat("\nNF2 - Number of enriched GO terms (all ontologies):", nrow(ego_nf2_all), "\n")


```


```{r ego}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

####################################################### 
# Visualize GO enrichment results
####################################################### 

# Bar plots
barplot(ego_smarce_all, showCategory = 10, title = "SMARCE1: Top GO Terms")
barplot(ego_nf2_all, showCategory = 10, title = "NF2: Top GO Terms")

# Dot plots (shows gene ratio and significance)
dotplot(ego_smarce_all, showCategory = 10, title = "SMARCE1: Top Go Terms")
dotplot(ego_nf2_all, showCategory = 10, title = "NF2: Top GO Terms")
```

## Top GO Terms

According to these plots, ECM genes  are significantly downregulated in SMARCE1-null cells
```{r topgo}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: hide
####################################################### 
# Explore genes in top enriched GO term
####################################################### 

# Get the 2nd top GO term for SMARCE1
top_go_smarce <- ego_smarce_all@result$ID[2]
cat("Top GO term for SMARCE1:", top_go_smarce, "\n")
cat("Description:", ego_smarce_all@result$Description[2], "\n")

# Get genes in this GO term
genes_in_go_smarce <- unlist(strsplit(ego_smarce_all@result$geneID[2], "/"))

# Get log2FC for these genes
fc_smarce <- res1$log2FoldChange[match(genes_in_go_smarce, sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res1)))]
padj_smarce <- res1$padj[match(genes_in_go_smarce, sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res1)))]

# Plot log2FC, sorted
ord_smarce <- order(fc_smarce)
plot(fc_smarce[ord_smarce],
     ylab = "log2FC of genes in top enriched GO term",
     col = (padj_smarce[ord_smarce] < 0.1) + 1,
     pch = 20, cex = 0.5,
     main = paste("SMARCE1:", ego_smarce_all@result$Description[2]))
abline(h = 0, lwd = 2, lty = 2, col = "gray")

# Repeat for NF2
top_go_nf2 <- ego_nf2_all@result$ID[2]
cat("\nTop GO term for NF2:", top_go_nf2, "\n")
cat("Description:", ego_nf2_all@result$Description[2], "\n")

genes_in_go_nf2 <- unlist(strsplit(ego_nf2_all@result$geneID[2], "/"))
fc_nf2 <- res2$log2FoldChange[match(genes_in_go_nf2, sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res2)))]
padj_nf2 <- res2$padj[match(genes_in_go_nf2, sub("\\..*", "", rownames(res2)))]

ord_nf2 <- order(fc_nf2)
plot(fc_nf2[ord_nf2],
     ylab = "log2FC of genes in top enriched GO term",
     col = (padj_nf2[ord_nf2] < 0.1) + 1,
     pch = 20, cex = 0.5,
     main = paste("NF2:", ego_nf2_all@result$Description[2]))
abline(h = 0, lwd = 2, lty = 2, col = "gray")
```


## GSEA Analysis

```{r gsea}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
####################################################### 
# GSEA (Gene Set Enrichment Analysis)
####################################################### 

library("clusterProfiler")

# For SMARCE1
# Extract log2 fold changes, only for genes that passed independent filtering
l2fcs_smarce <- as.data.frame(res1) %>%
  filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
  select(log2FoldChange)

# Put log2FCs in a vector, add gene IDs as names, sort
l2fcvec_smarce <- l2fcs_smarce[, 1]
names(l2fcvec_smarce) <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(l2fcs_smarce))  # Remove version numbers
l2fcvec_smarce <- sort(l2fcvec_smarce, decreasing = TRUE)

# Run GSEA
smarce_gsea <- gseGO(
  geneList = l2fcvec_smarce,
  OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
  keyType = "ENSEMBL",
  ont = "ALL",
  pvalueCutoff = 0.5,
  pAdjustMethod = "BH"
)

# View results
#head(smarce_gsea, 10)

# GSEA plot for top enriched pathway
gseaplot(smarce_gsea, by = "all", title = smarce_gsea$Description[1], geneSetID = 1)

# For NF2
l2fcs_nf2 <- as.data.frame(res2) %>%
  filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
  select(log2FoldChange)

l2fcvec_nf2 <- l2fcs_nf2[, 1]
names(l2fcvec_nf2) <- sub("\\..*", "", rownames(l2fcs_nf2))
l2fcvec_nf2 <- sort(l2fcvec_nf2, decreasing = TRUE)

nf2_gsea <- gseGO(
  geneList = l2fcvec_nf2,
  OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
  keyType = "ENSEMBL",
  ont = "ALL",
  pvalueCutoff = 0.5,
  pAdjustMethod = "BH"
)

#head(nf2_gsea, 10)

gseaplot(nf2_gsea, by = "all", title = nf2_gsea$Description[1], geneSetID = 1)

# Save GSEA results
write.csv(as.data.frame(smarce_gsea), "kadoch_SMARCE1_GSEA.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(as.data.frame(nf2_gsea), "kadoch_NF2_GSEA.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```


```{r enrichplot}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
# View SMARCE1 GSEA results as dataframe
#as.data.frame(smarce_gsea)

# View NF2 GSEA results as dataframe
#as.data.frame(nf2_gsea)

# Or to see just the top results
#head(as.data.frame(smarce_gsea), 20)
#head(as.data.frame(nf2_gsea), 20)
```

## Upset Plot GO


Upset plots were generated to visualize the overlap of genes across the top enriched GO terms for each condition. These plots show which genes are shared across multiple biological processes and which are specific to individual pathways.

```{r}
library("ggupset")
library("enrichplot")

library("ggupset")
library("enrichplot")

# Show fewer sets and adjust figure size
upsetplot(ego_smarce_all, n = 7)


# For NF2
p2 <- upsetplot(ego_nf2_all, n = 6)
p2 + theme(
  axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
  panel.grid = element_line(size = 10)
)
```


## Upset Plot GSEA 

```{r upsetplot}

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Then load enrichplot and try again

library("enrichplot")

# Upset plot for SMARCE1 GSEA
upsetplot(smarce_gsea)

# Upset plot for NF2 GSEA
upsetplot(nf2_gsea)


```


## Ridge Plots

The SMARCE1 ridgeplot revealed distinct expression patterns across enriched pathways. Developmental and structural pathways showed bimodal or broadly distributed expression, with genes spanning both upregulated and downregulated ranges. In contrast, NF2-null cells showed dramatically different pathway enrichments with strong directional bias. Nearly all enriched pathways displayed distributions shifted far to the right, indicating  upregulation:

```{r gsea-ridgeplot}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-cap: "GSEA ridgeplots showing expression distribution across top enriched pathways"



library(enrichplot)

# Ridgeplot for SMARCE1
ridgeplot(smarce_gsea, showCategory = 15, label_format = 40) +
  ggtitle("SMARCE1 GSEA: Expression Distribution")+
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9))

# Ridgeplot for NF2
ridgeplot(nf2_gsea, showCategory = 15, label_format = 40) +
  ggtitle("NF2 GSEA: Expression Distribution")+
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9))
```

## Gene Concept Network

These network plots help to visualize relationships between enriched GO terms based on shared genes. They show the functional relationships and clustering patterns among biological processes. 



```{r emapplot-clean}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6

library(enrichplot)
library(patchwork)

# Calculate similarity
ego_smarce_sim <- pairwise_termsim(ego_smarce_all)
ego_nf2_sim <- pairwise_termsim(ego_nf2_all)

# Plot
p1 <- emapplot(ego_smarce_sim, 
               showCategory = 20,
               cex_label_category = 0.6) +
  ggtitle("SMARCE1")

p2 <- emapplot(ego_nf2_sim, 
               showCategory = 20,
               cex_label_category = 0.6) +
  ggtitle("NF2")

p1 | p2
```

```{r output}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

####################################################### 
# Save results to organized directory structure
####################################################### 

# Create output directory
output_dir <- "05_results"
if (!dir.exists(output_dir)) {
  dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE)
}

# Set prefixes
outputPrefix1 <- file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_SMARCE1_DESeq2")
outputPrefix2 <- file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_NF2_DESeq2")

# Prepare results with normalized counts and gene symbols
resdata1 <- merge(
  as.data.frame(res_shrink1),
  as.data.frame(counts(dds, normalized = TRUE)),
  by = 'row.names', sort = FALSE
)
names(resdata1)[1] <- 'gene'

# Add gene symbols
resdata1 <- merge(resdata1, 
                  ann[, c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_symbol", "description")],
                  by.x = "gene",
                  by.y = "ensembl_gene_id",
                  all.x = TRUE)

resdata2 <- merge(
  as.data.frame(res_shrink2),
  as.data.frame(counts(dds, normalized = TRUE)),
  by = 'row.names', sort = FALSE
)
names(resdata2)[1] <- 'gene'

resdata2 <- merge(resdata2, 
                  ann[, c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_symbol", "description")],
                  by.x = "gene",
                  by.y = "ensembl_gene_id",
                  all.x = TRUE)

####################################################### 
# SMARCE1 Results
####################################################### 

# 1. ALL genes with results
write.csv(resdata1, 
          file = paste0(outputPrefix1, "_ALL_genes.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

# 2. Significant genes (padj < 0.1, no FC cutoff)
sig_genes1_all <- resdata1 %>% filter(padj < 0.1)
write.csv(sig_genes1_all, 
          file = paste0(outputPrefix1, "_significant_padj0.1.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

# 3. Stringent cutoff (padj < 0.01 AND |log2FC| > 1)
sig_genes1_stringent <- resdata1 %>% 
  filter(padj < 0.01 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1)
write.csv(sig_genes1_stringent, 
          file = paste0(outputPrefix1, "_significant_padj0.01_FC1.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

####################################################### 
# NF2 Results
####################################################### 

# 1. ALL genes with results
write.csv(resdata2, 
          file = paste0(outputPrefix2, "_ALL_genes.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

# 2. Significant genes (padj < 0.1, no FC cutoff)
sig_genes2_all <- resdata2 %>% filter(padj < 0.1)
write.csv(sig_genes2_all, 
          file = paste0(outputPrefix2, "_significant_padj0.1.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

# 3. Stringent cutoff (padj < 0.01 AND |log2FC| > 1)
sig_genes2_stringent <- resdata2 %>% 
  filter(padj < 0.01 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1)
write.csv(sig_genes2_stringent, 
          file = paste0(outputPrefix2, "_significant_padj0.01_FC1.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

####################################################### 
# Additional outputs
####################################################### 

# Normalized counts matrix
write.table(
  as.data.frame(counts(dds, normalized = TRUE)),
  file = file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_normalized_counts.txt"),
  sep = '\t'
)

# GO enrichment results
write.csv(as.data.frame(ego_smarce_all), 
          file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_SMARCE1_GO_all.csv"), 
          row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(as.data.frame(ego_nf2_all), 
          file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_NF2_GO_all.csv"), 
          row.names = FALSE)

# GSEA results
write.csv(as.data.frame(smarce_gsea), 
          file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_SMARCE1_GSEA.csv"), 
          row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(as.data.frame(nf2_gsea), 
          file.path(output_dir, "kadoch_NF2_GSEA.csv"), 
          row.names = FALSE)

# Print summary
cat("\n=== FILES SAVED ===\n")
cat("SMARCE1:\n")
cat("  - All genes:", nrow(resdata1), "\n")
cat("  - Significant (padj < 0.1):", nrow(sig_genes1_all), "\n")
cat("  - Stringent (padj < 0.01 & |FC| > 1):", nrow(sig_genes1_stringent), "\n\n")

cat("NF2:\n")
cat("  - All genes:", nrow(resdata2), "\n")
cat("  - Significant (padj < 0.1):", nrow(sig_genes2_all), "\n")
cat("  - Stringent (padj < 0.01 & |FC| > 1):", nrow(sig_genes2_stringent), "\n\n")

cat("All files saved to:", normalizePath(output_dir), "\n")
```


## Result Output Files

05_results/
├── kadoch_SMARCE1_DESeq2_ALL_genes.csv
├── kadoch_SMARCE1_DESeq2_significant_padj0.1.csv
├── kadoch_SMARCE1_DESeq2_significant_padj0.01_FC1.csv
├── kadoch_NF2_DESeq2_ALL_genes.csv
├── kadoch_NF2_DESeq2_significant_padj0.1.csv
├── kadoch_NF2_DESeq2_significant_padj0.01_FC1.csv
├── kadoch_normalized_counts.txt
├── kadoch_SMARCE1_GO_all.csv
├── kadoch_NF2_GO_all.csv
├── kadoch_SMARCE1_GSEA.csv
└── kadoch_NF2_GSEA.csv




# Discussion

In this section we will talk about the key findings of the RNA-seq analysis and compare it with the published data. 

Key Findings
1. Smarce1 Null cells hows a transcriptional signature that is distinct from WT cells. 
2. Loss of Smarce1 results in about half the genes upregulated and half downregulated, suggesting an overall transcriptional activation and repression that is not directional. 
3. NF2 null cells also show a similar pattern, however only 3456 genes were differentially expressed compared to Smarce Null cells which had 8037 genes differentially expressed. 
4. The PCA plot shows strong and distinct clustering of the Smarce Null and  nF2 null suggesting they are transcriptionally different although both are involved in tumorogenesis. 

Our analysis identified a larger number of differentially expressed genes (8,037 vs 3,456 for SMARCE1) compared to the original publication where 1,684 genes were significantly downregulated1,772 genes were significantly upregulated. This is likely due to differences in statistical thresholds (padj < 0.05  compared to stricter cut offs), the software versions (DESeq2 vs edgeR v3.12.1) and filtering approaches. However, the ratio of upregulated to downregulated genes remained consistent with the published results, confirming the balanced transcriptional response to SMARCE1 loss.
